card.title: Why Songs Get Stuck in Your Head
card.teaser: That chorus you can't stop humming? Scientists call it an earworm, and they've figured out exactly why your brain won't let go.
card.coverImagePrompt: A translucent brain silhouette with a musical note spiraling inside like a coiled worm, vibrant purple and pink, surreal and artistic

lessons.title: The song you didn't ask for
lessons.body: It's 2 AM. You're trying to sleep. And somewhere in the back of your head, the chorus to a song you haven't heard in a decade keeps looping. You didn't play it. You didn't even think about it. But it's there now, and it refuses to leave. Welcome to the wonderful, maddening world of earworms.

visual.imagePrompt: A person lying awake in bed at night with musical notes floating above their head in a dreamy spiral, moonlight through curtains

lessons.title: Involuntary musical imagery
lessons.body: The technical term is involuntary musical imagery, or INMI. Studies suggest over 90 percent of people experience earworms weekly. They usually last from a few minutes to a few hours, though occasional sufferers have reported loops lasting days. The songs are almost never entire tracks — they're short, catchy fragments, usually the chorus or hook.

keyTerms.definition: The scientific name for a song spontaneously playing in your head
A catchy melodic fragment that repeats involuntarily in the mind

visual.imagePrompt: A looping ribbon of musical notation curling around itself like an infinity symbol, soft watercolor style

lessons.title: Why your brain loves a loop
lessons.body: The auditory cortex — the part of your brain that processes sound — doesn't quit when a song ends. It keeps firing in echo patterns, and short catchy phrases fit perfectly inside short-term memory. Songs with predictable melodic contours and unusual intervals seem to stick hardest. The brain treats an incomplete loop like an unfinished sentence: it keeps trying to 'close' it, and replaying is how.

visual.imagePrompt: A colorful glowing brain cross-section with sound waves echoing through the auditory cortex, artistic neuroscience

lessons.title: The worst offenders, measured
lessons.body: Researchers at Durham University analyzed thousands of reported earworms and found some universal champions: Lady Gaga's 'Bad Romance,' Journey's 'Don't Stop Believin',' and the jingle for Kylie Minogue's 'Can't Get You Out of My Head.' The common pattern — a fast tempo, repetitive melodic shape, and one surprising interval — is essentially the chemical formula for stickiness.
lessons.funFact: Chewing gum has been shown in controlled studies to reduce earworms, probably because it occupies the same brain regions used for subvocal singing.

visual.imagePrompt: A playful chart of album covers stacked like a pop music leaderboard, colorful retro design

lessons.title: How to escape the loop
lessons.body: If an earworm won't budge, research suggests a few reliable tricks. Listen to the song all the way through — closure often ends the loop. Engage in a different cognitive task like a crossword or counting backwards. Or try the gum trick. What rarely works? Trying to actively suppress it. Like a jack-in-the-box, the more you push down, the harder it springs back.

quiz.question: What is the scientific name for a song stuck in your head?
quiz.explanation: Researchers call the phenomenon involuntary musical imagery, or INMI. 'Earworm' is the popular term.

quiz.question: What has been shown to reduce earworms in controlled studies?
quiz.explanation: Chewing gum occupies brain regions involved in subvocal singing, interfering with the loop.

quiz.question: Why can listening to a song all the way through sometimes stop an earworm?
options: It tires out the auditory cortex
It provides the 'closure' the brain was seeking
It replaces the melody with lyrics
quiz.explanation: The brain treats unfinished loops like unfinished sentences. Hearing the song completed gives the closure it was chasing.

sources.title: Dissecting an Earworm: Melodic Features and Song Popularity Predict Involuntary Musical Imagery
sources.publisher: Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts

sources.title: Musicophilia: Tales of Music and the Brain
